segments.purgatorio-7-g11: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'in that way'. Rispuose is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd person singular of rispondere ('to answer'); the form rispuose is a poetic/older variant of rispose. Allora is an adverb 'then' indicating time. Il duca mio is a noun phrase 'my duke/leader' (duca here functioning as 'guide' in the poem) with il (definite article) + duca (noun) + mio (possessive adjective 'my').
segments.purgatorio-7-g12: Qual è colui functions as a comparative/illustrative opening ('like one who' / 'as is he who'); Qual here introduces the simile. È is the present indicative of essere ('is'). Colui is the demonstrative pronoun 'the one' / 'he who'. Che introduces the relative clause 'who'. Cosa means 'thing' or 'something' and is the direct object of vede. Innanzi sé means 'before himself' (innanzi = before/in front of; sé = himself). Sùbita is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying vede. Vede is present indicative 'sees'. Ond' is an archaic/truncated form of 'onde' ('whereof/whereat/whence') linking to the result 'he marvels', e is 'and', si is the reflexive pronoun used with maravigliare to form 'si maraviglia' (he is astonished), and maraviglia is present indicative 'marvels / is astonished'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g13: Che resumes the relative idea 'who'. Crede is present indicative of credere ('he believes' / 'he thinks'); e non is the simple coordinate 'and not' expressing a contradictory uncertainty — i.e., 'who both believes and does not (believe)'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g14: Dicendo is the present gerund of dire ('saying'), indicating simultaneous speech; it introduces what the person utters. Ella is the feminine pronoun 'she' (here used as the subject of the reported clause). È is present indicative of essere ('is'), starting the reported clause 'She is...'. The clause is left unfinished by the quotation mark in the manuscript.
segments.purgatorio-7-g15: This segment contains only a punctuation mark (a period) terminating the sentence; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-7-g16: This segment is only a punctuation mark (a period) and carries no words to explain.
segments.purgatorio-7-g17: "non" is the standard sentential negator placed before the verb; "è" is the third-person singular present (here: literary/simple present) of essere, meaning "is." Together they form the simple negative statement "is not."
segments.purgatorio-7-g18: This segment contains only punctuation (a single period) with no lexical material to analyze; it marks a pause or line break in the poem.
segments.purgatorio-7-g19: This segment is likewise only punctuation (a single period) and has no words to analyze; it functions as a pause or end-mark in the verse.
segments.purgatorio-7-g20: Punctuation: the closing quotation mark and comma indicate the end of direct speech and continue the narrative voice.
segments.purgatorio-7-g21: "tal" (= tale) means "such" or "so"; "parve" is the literary/perfect (passato remoto) 3rd-person singular of parere, here meaning "seemed" or "appeared"; "quelli" is used here as an indirect/dative pronoun (to him), placed after the verb in poetic/archaic word order, i.e. "(he) seemed such to him."
segments.purgatorio-7-g22: "e poi" = "and then"; "chinò" is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of chinare meaning "he lowered/bowed"; "le ciglia" literal "the eyelashes/eyelids" is a common idiom for "his eyes" (i.e. he let his gaze fall).
segments.purgatorio-7-g23: "e umilmente" = "and humbly" (adverb + conjunction); "ritornò" is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of ritornare, "he returned/turned back"; "ver’" is a poetic elision of "verso" meaning "toward" (the apostrophe marks the elision); "lui" is the indirect object pronoun "him."
segments.purgatorio-7-g24: "e abbracciòl" is abbracciò + enclitic object pronoun -l (lo), i.e. "and he embraced him"; "là" = "there"; "’ve" = poetic form of "ove"/"ove" meaning "where"; "’l minor" = "the younger one" (minor used substantively); "s’" is the reflexive clitic (si) and "appiglia" is present tense 3rd-person singular of appigliarsi meaning "clings/takes hold (of)." The clause means he embraced him at the spot where the younger one was clinging.
segments.purgatorio-7-g1: "Poscia che" here is a temporal conjunction meaning "after"; the clause that follows is subordinate. "l’accoglienze oneste e liete" is the feminine plural subject (article l' + accoglienze) with two coordinating adjectives; "furo" is an archaic past (passato remoto) form of essere used with the past participle "iterate" (iterare → iterate) to form a passive: "were repeated." "tre e quattro volte" is an idiomatic way of saying "three or four times" (a counted repetition).
segments.purgatorio-7-g2: "Sordel" is the proper name subject. "si trasse" is a pronominal use of trarre in the passato remoto (trasse) with the reflexive pronoun "si"; here it means "drew back/withdrew/stepped back." The verb is in the main clause following the temporal subordinate.
segments.purgatorio-7-g3: "e" is the coordinating conjunction "and" linking the action of withdrawing to speaking; "disse" is the passato remoto (third person singular) of dire, introducing the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-7-g4: "Voi" is the vocative/plural (or polite) second-person pronoun used in direct address; the capitalization is respectful and marks direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-7-g5: "chi" is the interrogative pronoun "who"; "siete" is the present indicative, second-person plural of essere (you are) — used here in direct address (plural or polite singular). Together they form the question "who are you?"
segments.purgatorio-7-g6: A single punctuation mark (full stop/period) in the text — no lexical items to analyze. It functions as the end of a line or a brief pause in the poem.
segments.purgatorio-7-g7: "Anzi che" here introduces a temporal clause meaning "before." "fosser volte" is an archaic/periphrastic form: "fosser" is an older form of the pluperfect/subjunctive auxiliary (fossero/furono in older usage) combined with the past participle "volte" (from volgere, "turned"); together they give a past oriented passive/reciprocal sense "had been turned" or "had turned". "l’anime degne di salire a Dio" is the feminine plural subject (the souls) qualified by "degne" + infinitive phrase "di salire a Dio" (worthy to ascend to God).
segments.purgatorio-7-g8: "fur" is the archaic past auxiliary (fur[ono]) meaning "were"; word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: subject "l’ossa mie" (my bones) follows the auxiliary. "per Ottavian" uses "per" in the sense "by" to mark the agent (Octavian) in this passive construction. "sepolte" is the past participle (feminine plural) agreeing with "ossa."
segments.purgatorio-7-g9: "Io" is the emphatic first-person pronoun "I"; "son" is the archaic short form of "sono" (present indicative, I am); "Virgilio" is the name (predicate nominative). The clause is a plain self-identification.
segments.purgatorio-7-g10: "e" = "and." "per null’ altro" is an elided form meaning "for no other" (null' = nulla/nessun); "rio" is an adjective meaning "wicked" or "guilty" used substantively here (no other guilt/wickedness). "lo ciel perdei" literally "I lost the heaven(s)" — "perdei" is passato remoto first person singular of perdere (the subject "I" is understood from the previous clause). "che" here functions as "than/except" (introducing the limiting cause). "per non aver fé" = "for not having faith," with "aver" infinitive and "fé" (archaic spelling of fede) meaning faith — the reason given for his losing heaven.
segments.purgatorio-7-g25: A vocative exclamation: O + noun phrase. O (interjection) addresses the addressee; gloria di Latin is a genitive noun phrase ‘glory of the Latins’ (Latin = Latins/Latin people) used as a title.
segments.purgatorio-7-g26: Simple reporting verb in passato remoto: disse = ‘he said.’ It introduces the speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-7-g27: per cui = ‘through/by whom’; mostrò is passato remoto ‘showed/revealed’; ciò che potea = ‘what it could’ with potea an imperfect (archaic spelling of poteva) expressing ability in the past; la lingua nostra = ‘our tongue/our language.’ Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (mostrò ... la lingua nostra).
segments.purgatorio-7-g28: Another vocative phrase: o = ‘O/Oh’; pregio etterno = ‘eternal worth/eternal honor’; del loco = ‘of the place’; ond' io fui = archaic/poetic ‘from which I was’ (ond’ = whence); fui is passato remoto (‘I was’/‘I came from’).
segments.purgatorio-7-g29: A direct question in the present: qual merito o qual grazia = ‘what merit or what grace’; mi ti mostra = literal clitic order ‘shows you to me’ (mi = indirect object ‘to me’, ti = direct object ‘you’); mostra is present indicative ‘shows’.
segments.purgatorio-7-g30: Conditional clause S’io = se io (‘if I’) with son (poetic short form of sono) = ‘I am’; d’udir = di + infinitive udire = ‘to hear’ (contracted); le tue parole = ‘your words’; degno = predicate adjective ‘worthy.’ The adjective is placed after the noun phrase by poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-7-g31: Imperative dimmi = ‘tell me’; se vien = ‘if/whether you come’ (se = whether/if, vien = present of venire); d’inferno = di + inferno = ‘from Hell.’
segments.purgatorio-7-g32: e = ‘and’; di qual chiostra = ‘from which cloister’ (di = from/of, qual = which/what, chiostra = cloister/monastic enclosure or company).
segments.purgatorio-7-g33: Per tutt’ i cerchi = ‘through all the circles’ (Per = through, tutt’ = poetic truncation of tutti = all); del dolente regno = ‘of the sorrowful/doleful realm’ (genitive). Note: cerchi here means ‘circles’ (Dante’s circles of Hell), though the provided lemma senses are for a different wordform.
segments.purgatorio-7-g34: rispuose (archaic spelling of rispose) = passato remoto ‘answered’; lui = ‘he’ or ‘to him’ (here ‘he answered’).
segments.purgatorio-7-g35: son io di qua venuto = ‘I have come from this side/ I came from here’: son = poetic short form of sono (present), di qua = ‘from here/this side’, venuto = past participle used with the copula to indicate coming (poetic inversion places io after son).
segments.purgatorio-7-g36: virtù del ciel = ‘a power/virtue of Heaven’ (virtù = power/virtue; ciel = heaven); mi mosse = passato remoto of muovere literally ‘moved me’ (caused me to move/come).
segments.purgatorio-7-g37: e con lei vegno = ‘and with it/her I come’: con lei = ‘with it/her’ (lei refers back to virtù, feminine), vegno = present ‘I come’ (poetic form of vengo).
segments.purgatorio-7-g38: Non per far is an elliptical prepositional phrase: 'non' negates the following construction, 'per' + infinitive often expresses means or cause (here 'by' or 'by means of'), and 'far' is the archaic/poetic infinitive form of fare ('to do'). Together the phrase means 'not by doing'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g39: This full clause contrasts with the previous phrase: 'ma' (but) introduces the contrast; 'per non fare' again uses 'per' + infinitive with negation to express cause ('by not doing'); 'ho perduto' is present perfect (ho + past participle) meaning 'I have lost'; 'a veder' is the infinitive with preposition 'a' meaning 'to see' or 'the chance to see' (common after perduto in this idiom); 'l’alto Sol' is the direct object ('the high Sun'); the following relative clauses 'che tu disiri' ('that you desire') uses the 2nd-person form disiri (an archaic/poetic form corresponding to moderno 'desideri'); 'e che fu tardi per me conosciuto' uses passato remoto 'fu' + past participle 'conosciuto' in a passive or result sense meaning 'that became/was known to me' with 'tardi' ('late') indicating it happened too late for the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-7-g54: Ma is the adversative 'but'. Vedi is second-person present of vedere used here as an exhortation (English 'see' / 'look'): 'look/notice'. Già = 'already'. Come = 'how' introducing the clause describing the manner/state. Dichina is 3rd-person singular present of the archaic verb dichinare 'to incline/decline', so 'the day declines'; il giorno is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word-order.
segments.purgatorio-7-g55: E = 'and'. Andar is the infinitive 'to go' (here the notion 'to go up'). Sù is the adverb 'up'. Di notte = 'at night' (di + noun expressing time). Non + si + puote forms an impersonal construction: si is the impersonal/clitic 'one/it', and puote is an archaic form of potere (3rd person singular), so non si puote = 'one cannot' / 'it is not possible'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g56: Però = 'therefore' / 'so'. È is 3rd-person singular present of essere 'is'. Buon pensar is a compact noun phrase meaning 'a good thought/plan' (buon qualifies the infinitival noun pensar). Di introduces the object of that thinking ('di bel soggiorno' = 'about a pleasant lodging'). Bel is the aphetic form of bello 'beautiful/fine/pleasant'. Soggiorno = 'stay'/'lodging'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g57: Anime = 'souls' (subject). Sono is 3rd-person plural present of essere 'are' (locative/existential: there are). A destra = 'to the right' (directional). Qua = 'here'. Remote = adjective 'remote/removed/distant' describing their position (i.e. set a little aside).
segments.purgatorio-7-g58: Se = 'if' (conditional conjunction). Mi = clitic 'to me'/'me' (indirect object). Consenti is 2nd-person singular present of consentire 'you consent / you agree' (spoken to the pilgrim). The whole phrase = 'if you agree to me' / 'if you permit me'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g59: Io = emphatic subject 'I'. Ti = clitic 'you' (direct object). Merrò is a future 1st-person form (archaic/spelling variant) meaning 'I will lead/bring' (compare modern menar/condurre); it expresses a future action. Ad = preposition 'to'. Esse here is an archaic/dialectal object pronoun 'them' (not the infinitive esser(e)); 'ad esse' = 'to them'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g60: E = 'and'. Non sanza = lit. 'not without', so the phrase 'non sanza diletto' means 'with pleasure' or 'not without delight'. Diletto = 'pleasure/delight'. Ti = 'you'. Fier is an archaic/poetic form related to Latin fieri ('to become / to be made'); here it functions to yield a passive/result meaning 'you will be made/shall become'. Note = adjective from noto 'known' — 'ti fier note' therefore = 'you will be made known/acknowledged' (i.e. you will be recognized), with the preceding 'non sanza diletto' adding that this will be to their/your delight. Several forms are archaic and reflect older Italian usage.
segments.purgatorio-7-g61: Com' is the contracted form of come = 'how' (the apostrophe marks elision of the final vowel). È is 3rd-person singular present of essere 'is'. Ciò = demonstrative pronoun 'that/this'. The short question asks for clarification: 'How is that?' / 'What do you mean by that?'
segments.purgatorio-7-g62: This segment is the comma punctuation separating speech and the following narrative comment; it has no lexical items.
segments.purgatorio-7-g63: Fu is the passato remoto (3rd-person singular) of essere used as auxiliary here meaning 'was'. Risposto is the past participle of rispondere 'to answer': together fu risposto = 'was answered' (a passive or impersonal narrative tag: 'it was answered' / 'he answered' in reported speech).
segments.purgatorio-7-g155: Quel che introduces a relative clause meaning "the one who"; quel is a pronominal determiner/pronoun ('the one') and che is the relative pronoun 'who/which'. Più basso is a comparative phrase (più + adjective) here functioning as a single adjectival idea "lower/lowest." Tra costor places the subject among a group: tra = 'among' and costor is an archaic demonstrative meaning 'these people/these ones.' S’atterra is a reflexive verb form: s’ = si (reflexive) cliticized before a vowel, and atterra is present indicative 3rd person singular of atterrare meaning here 'alight/settle/sit' (poetic use), so the clause reads literally "the one who settles himself lower among these."
segments.purgatorio-7-g156: Guardando is the gerund of guardare and means 'looking' or 'while looking'; the gerund here expresses a simultaneous action modifying the subject of the main clause. In + suso is an older locative phrase meaning 'up' or 'upward' (suso = 'up above'), so guardando in suso = 'looking upward.' The phrase is adverbial, qualifying how the person is seated.
segments.purgatorio-7-g157: È is the present indicative 3rd person singular of essere functioning as the copula 'is.' Guiglielmo marchese is a name plus title in apposition (no article), so the structure is simple subject-predicate: 'is William the Marquis.' The noun marchese follows the proper name as a title or identification.
segments.purgatorio-7-g158: Per cui begins a causal/relative phrase meaning 'by/for whom' with cui referring back to Guiglielmo. The coordinate expression e Alessandria e la sua guerra is the grammatical subject of fa: Dante stacks two causes with repeated e (and) — 'both Alessandria and his war.' Fa is the 3rd person singular of fare used causatively ('makes/causes') and takes the infinitive pianger ('to weep') as its complement: fa pianger = 'causes to weep' or 'makes ... weep.' Monferrato e Canavese are the objects (places made to weep), so the whole means 'by whom both Alessandria and his war make Monferrato and Canavese weep.' Note that sua agrees with Guiglielmo ('his war').
segments.purgatorio-7-g40: Subject–verb order is simple but somewhat inverted for emphasis: Luogo (subject) + è (present tense of essere). The two adverbs là giù ('there down/below') are placed after the verb for poetic stress. The predicate non tristo di martìri uses non + adjective (tristo) and a prepositional phrase di martìri modifying tristo: 'not sorrowful because of torments.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g41: ma is an adversative conjunction ('but'); di tenebre solo is a prepositional phrase where solo follows the noun for emphasis ('of darkness alone' / 'only of darkness'), with solo functioning as an adjective/adverb restricting tenebre.
segments.purgatorio-7-g42: ove introduces a relative/locative clause ('where'); i lamenti is the plural subject. suonan is 3rd-person plural present of suonare meaning 'sound'/'ring'. come introduces a comparison ('like'), so come guai = 'like cries of woe.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g43: ma ('but') contrasts with the previous clause; son is the archaic/poetic form of sono (present indicative 'are'). sospiri is a noun ('sighs') completing the predicate: 'but are sighs.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g44: Quivi sto io = 'there I stand' (quivi locative adverb + sto first-person present). coi pargoli innocenti is a prepositional phrase with coi (con + il) indicating accompaniment. dai denti morsi de la morte avante is an ablative/agent-like phrase: morsi is a past participle ('bitten'), so 'bitten by the teeth of death'; avante ('before') locates that action in time. The relative clause che fosser ... essenti uses fosser (archaic subjunctive/imperfect of essere) with essenti to express the state 'who were (or had been) exempt/removed from human guilt.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g45: quivi sto io con quei che = 'there I stand with those who'. le tre sante virtù names the three holy virtues (le + feminine plural sante + virtù). non si vestiro is a negative statement with si as a reflexive clitic and vestiro an archaic past form (Dantean spelling) meaning 'they clothed themselves' (i.e. 'did not clothe themselves in the three virtues').
segments.purgatorio-7-g46: e sanza vizio ('and without vice') qualifies the following verbs. conobber is an archaic past form (conobbero) 'they knew/knew of', with l’altre as its object ('the other [virtues]'). e seguir uses seguir in sense 'follow'; tutte quante ('all of them') pluralizes and emphasizes completeness. The two verbs relate to the same subject introduced earlier (quei).
segments.purgatorio-7-g47: Ma se tu sai e puoi is a conditional proviso: se ('if') introduces the clause; sai and puoi are both present indicative second-person singular ('you know' and 'you can'), coordinated by e. The speaker appeals directly to the addressee's knowledge and power.
segments.purgatorio-7-g48: alcuno indizio dà noi = 'give us some sign' (dà is present indicative 'gives/give'). per che introduces purpose or means ('by which' / 'so that'), and the subordinate uses venir possiam with possiam in the present subjunctive to express purpose/result ('that we may come'); più tosto is a comparative adverbial phrase 'more quickly/sooner.' là dove introduces the destination clause 'where Purgatory has its true/rightful beginning' (ha dritto inizio).
segments.purgatorio-7-g49: Rispuose is the passato remoto (simple past) third person singular of rispondere: 'he/she/it replied' (typical narrative tense in Dante). The colon that follows introduces the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-7-g50: 'Loco certo non c’è posto' is an existential statement: 'Loco certo' ('a definite/certain place') is fronted for emphasis; 'non c’ è' = 'there is not' (ci as the locative 'there' combined with è); 'posto' is the noun 'place/space'—together 'There certainly is no place.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g51: 'licito m’è andar suso e intorno' shows Dantean inversion for meter: the neutral order would be 'm’è licito andar suso e intorno' meaning 'it is permitted to me to go up and around.' 'licito' is an adjective ('permissible'); 'm’' (mi) is the dative clitic 'to me'; 'andar' is the infinitive 'to go' (archaic spelling 'ir' appears elsewhere), 'suso' is an archaic/adverbial form meaning 'up', and 'intorno' 'around.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g52: 'per quanto ir posso' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'as far as I can' or 'to the extent that I can go': 'per quanto' here functions idiomatically ('as far/as much as'), 'ir' is an archaic infinitive 'to go', and 'posso' is present indicative 'I can'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g53: 'a guida mi t’accosto' means 'I come close to you as a guide' or 'I offer myself to you as a guide.' 'a guida' indicates role or purpose ('as a guide'); the clitic sequence 'mi t’' places the reflexive/indirect 'mi' ('myself'/'to me' depending on nuance) before 't’' ('you'), and 'accosto' is present indicative 'I approach' or 'I draw near.' The sense is that the speaker will place himself alongside the addressee to guide him.
segments.purgatorio-7-g64: 'Chi' is an indefinite relative/pronoun here meaning 'whoever'; 'volesse' is the imperfect/subjunctive used for a polite or hypothetical wish ('wished'/'would wish'); it governs the infinitive 'salir' (archaic form of 'salire', 'to climb/ascend'). 'Di notte' is a temporal phrase 'at night' placed after the verb phrase.
segments.purgatorio-7-g65: 'Fora' is an archaic conditional form (from essere) meaning 'would be'. 'Elli' is an older/emphatic subject pronoun (egli/he) placed before the participle. 'Impedito' is a past participle used adjectivally ('hindered/ prevented'). 'D'altrui' = 'by others' (di + altrui). Word order (pronoun + participle) is typical of older Italian.
segments.purgatorio-7-g66: 'O' = 'or' introducing the alternative; 'non sarria' contains 'sarria', an archaic/poetic conditional form meaning roughly 'would be' (here used impersonally: 'would it not be'). 'Ché' introduces the subordinate clause ('that'/'that it be the case that'), and 'non potesse' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'potere' used in this hypothetical sense ('that he could not'). The whole phrase asks whether the reason is inability rather than others' prevention.
segments.purgatorio-7-g67: This segment is punctuation only (a full stop).
segments.purgatorio-7-g68: Conjunction 'E' = 'and'. '’l' is the contracted article 'il' before the adjective; 'buon' (prepositive adjective) modifies the proper name 'Sordello'. 'In terra' = 'on the ground'. 'Fregò' is passato remoto (simple past) of 'fregare' meaning 'he rubbed' here. '’l dito' = 'the finger' (article + noun); word order mirrors spoken/narrative style.
segments.purgatorio-7-g69: 'Dicendo' is the gerund of 'dire' and functions adverbially: 'saying' (introducing his spoken words). It links the action just described to the direct speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-7-g70: 'Vedi' is second-person singular present ('you see?' or 'see!') here used as an interjectional question addressed to the hearers: 'See?' It can be read as either present indicative interrogative or an imperative-like exclamation.
segments.purgatorio-7-g71: 'Sola questa riga' = 'this sole/only ridge/line' (sola prepositive 'only'). 'Non varcheresti' is the present conditional, 2nd person singular: 'you would not cross/pass'. 'Dopo 'l sol partito' is a temporal phrase: 'after the sun has departed/after sunset' — 'partito' is a past participle used adverbially ('having set/left').
segments.purgatorio-7-g72: 'Non però ch’' = 'not that' (negation + 'però' introducing the explanatory subordinate). 'Altra cosa' = 'any other thing'; 'desse' is the imperfect/subjunctive of 'dare' used impersonally meaning 'would give' or 'would cause' here; 'briga' = 'trouble/difficulty'. The clause denies that anything else would cause trouble.
segments.purgatorio-7-g73: This short clause ('che la notturna tenebra') names the real cause: 'che' here is the relative/conjunction linking to the prior negation, and 'la notturna tenebra' = 'the nocturnal darkness' (subject of the next thought).
segments.purgatorio-7-g74: 'Ad ir suso' is an infinitival phrase with 'ad' + infinitive (archaic usage) meaning 'to go up there'; 'ir' is the archaic infinitive 'to go' (cf. 'andare'), and 'suso' is an adverb 'up/above'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g75: 'Quella' (that one = the darkness just named) is the subject. 'Col' = 'con il' ('with the'); 'nonpoder' is a fused nominative/abstract infinitive 'non poter' (lack of ability/impotence) treated as a noun ('the inability'). 'La voglia' is the direct object ('the desire/will'), and 'intriga' is present indicative 3rd person singular: 'entangles/entices/troubles.' The clause says the darkness, together with inability, entangles the desire.
segments.purgatorio-7-g141: 'Rade volte' is a poetic adverbial phrase meaning 'rarely' or 'seldom' (rado → rare); 'risurge' is 3rd person singular present indicative of risorgere, 'resurges/appears again'. 'Per li rami' is a metaphorical locative 'through the branches' (rami = family branches/lines) and uses the archaic plural article 'li' instead of modern 'i'. 'L’ umana probitate' (elided 'la') is the feminine subject 'human probity'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g142: This clause links to the next by stating purpose/will: 'e questo vole' = 'and this he wills/means' with 'vole' as 3rd person singular of volere (he desires/wills). 'Quei che' is a demonstrative + relative construction meaning 'he who' or 'those who'; 'la dà' has 'la' as a feminine direct object pronoun (referring back to 'probitate') and 'dà' is present indicative of dare ('gives').
segments.purgatorio-7-g143: 'Perché' here introduces purpose/result rather than a simple question, so 'so that'/'in order that'. 'Da lui' = 'from him' (meaning 'named after him'), placed before the verb for emphasis. 'Si chiami' is a reflexive/passive construction with the present subjunctive (chiamare → si chiami) giving a purpose sense: 'may be called' or 'be called (after him)'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g144: 'Anche' = 'also'; 'al nasuto' = 'to the long‑nosed one' (nasuto is an epithet 'large‑nosed'). 'Vanno mie parole' literally 'my words go' (= 'my words apply/are addressed'). 'Non men ch’a l’altro' is a comparative idiom 'no less than to the other' where 'non men' = 'no less' and 'ch’' is the elided 'che' functioning as 'than' in the comparison.
segments.purgatorio-7-g145: A single-name vocative: 'Pier' addresses or names the person; no grammar beyond the proper name form.
segments.purgatorio-7-g146: 'Che con lui canta' is a relative clause modifying Pier: 'who sings with him.' 'Che' = 'who/which', 'con' = 'with', 'lui' = 'him', and 'canta' is 3rd person singular present of cantare 'sings'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g147: 'Onde' here means 'wherefore/therefore' or 'by which fact' introducing the consequence: 'hence Apulia and Provence already lament.' 'Puglia e Proenza' are place/national references (Apulia and Provence). 'Già si dole' uses the pronominal verb 'dolersi' in present indicative with 'già' = 'already' meaning 'already lament/complain.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g148: Poetic inversion: 'Tant' è ... minor la pianta' = 'So much is the plant smaller of his seed' → 'The offspring's plant is so much smaller.' 'Tant’ è' = 'so much is'; 'del seme suo' = 'of his seed/offspring' (seme = offspring here); 'minor' = comparative 'smaller/lesser'; 'la pianta' = 'the plant' (metaphor for branch/line).
segments.purgatorio-7-g149: 'Quanto' begins the comparative clause 'as much as' and pairs with the preceding 'tant' ... 'quanto' construction: 'so much ... as'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g150: 'Più che Beatrice e Margherita' is a comparative phrase 'more than Beatrice and Margherita.' 'Più' = 'more'; 'che' is used here as 'than' in comparisons; the two names are the standards of comparison.
segments.purgatorio-7-g151: 'Costanza di marito ancor si vanta' reads 'Costanza still boasts of a husband.' 'Costanza' is a proper name (Constance); 'di marito' = 'of (a) husband' (object of vanta); 'ancor' = archaic 'ancora' = 'still'; 'si vanta' is the pronominal verb vantarsi 'to boast'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g152: 'Vedete' is the 2nd person plural imperative/present indicative 'you see' (used as 'look'/'observe'). 'Il re de la semplice vita' = 'the king of the simple life' where 'de la' is the archaic form of 'della'. 'Seder là solo' uses the infinitive 'seder' (poetic use instead of a participle) meaning 'to sit' or 'sitting there alone'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g153: A nominative apposition naming the king: 'Arrigo d’Inghilterra' = 'Henry of England.' 'd’' is the elided form of 'di'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g154: 'Questi ha ne’ rami suoi migliore uscita' means 'this one has a better outcome in his branches.' 'Questi' = 'this one'; 'ha' is present of avere; 'ne’' (= 'ne i' contracted) = 'in them/in' referring to 'rami' (branches); 'rami suoi' = 'his branches'; 'migliore uscita' = 'a better outcome/result' (migliore = comparative/adjective modifying uscita).
segments.purgatorio-7-g76: This is an impersonal/modal construction. 'Ben' is an adverbial intensifier modifying the verb; 'si poria' uses 'si' impersonally with the conditional/possibility verb 'poria' (an older form meaning 'could/it would be possible'). The two infinitives 'tornare' and 'passeggiar' follow to express the potential actions; 'con lei' is a prepositional phrase 'with her', and 'in giuso' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'downward'. 'Errando' is a present participle/gerund ('wandering') that modifies 'passeggiar' and appears after the phrase it qualifies, a common poetic placement.
segments.purgatorio-7-g77: 'Mentre che' is a temporal expression meaning 'while/as long as'; 'che' is the subordinating particle introducing the clause. 'L'orizzonte' is the subject (the horizon), and 'il dì' is the direct object of 'tien' ('tien' = third-person singular present of 'tenere', here 'keeps/holds'). 'Chiuso' is a predicative adjective meaning 'closed/hidden' describing 'il dì'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g78: 'Allora' is a temporal adverb ('then'); 'il mio segnor' is the nominative phrase 'my lord' (segnor = lord, archaic spelling). The comma indicates the pause before the participial phrase that follows.
segments.purgatorio-7-g79: 'Quasi ammirando' is a circumstantial participial phrase: 'quasi' here means 'as if' or 'seemingly', and 'ammirando' is the gerund/participle of 'ammirare' ('admiring' or 'marveling'), describing the manner in which 'my lord' spoke.
segments.purgatorio-7-g80: 'Menane' is an archaic imperative form made of 'mena' (from 'menare' = 'to lead') plus the enclitic pronoun '-ne' ('of it/from there' or 'us/there' in context). Grammatically it's a command 'Lead us/lead on from here.' The form and clitic reflect older Italian/pronoun placement.
segments.purgatorio-7-g81: 'Disse' is the passato remoto (simple past) of 'dire' 'to say' and functions as the reporting verb introducing the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-7-g82: This is the reported content. 'Dunque' is an adverb 'then/therefore'; 'là 've' = 'there where' (là = there, 've' = dove/where); 'dici' is second-person present 'you say'. 'Ch’aver si può' is an impersonal construction: 'ch’' = 'that', 'aver' = infinitive 'to have', 'si' impersonal 'one', and 'può' = 'can/is able to' — together 'that one can have'. 'Diletto' is the noun 'delight', and 'dimorando' is a gerund/participle 'by dwelling/in staying' that specifies how the delight is had.
segments.purgatorio-7-g83: This is a circumstantial phrase. 'Poco allungati' is a past participle phrase meaning 'having gone on a little' or 'a little advanced'; it qualifies the subject. 'C’eravam' is a contracted form of 'ci eravamo' (we were there/there we were). 'Di lici' is an old locative/partitive phrase — the exact lexical sense of 'lici' is dialectal/archaic; here it indicates the place/feature along which they had moved.
segments.purgatorio-7-g84: 'Quand’ io m’accorsi' = 'When I perceived/noticed'. The clitic 'm’' (mi) is reflexive and part of 'm'accorsi' (past 'I noticed'). 'Che' introduces the subordinate clause 'that the mountain was scemo'; 'era' is imperfect 'was'. The adjective 'scemo' in this context means 'bare/stripped' (archaic sense).
segments.purgatorio-7-g85: 'A guisa che' is a concessive/comparative phrase meaning 'in such a way that' or 'so that'; 'i vallon' (the ravines/large valleys) is the subject of the following verb; 'li sceman' is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'they make it (the mountain) bare'—here the provided lemma senses don't align, so the verb functions causatively; 'quici' is an adverb 'here'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g86: 'Colà' is a deictic adverb meaning 'there' or 'yonder', and begins the quoted speech as a simple locative pointer.
segments.purgatorio-7-g87: 'Disse' (he/she said) is the narration verb; 'quell’ ombra' = 'that shade/shadow' is the nominative subject (ombra = shade/ghost/phantom). The article enclitic 'quell’' marks 'that'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g88: 'N’anderemo' is 'ne andremo' (we will go from/away from there) with the clitic 'ne' attached to the future 'anderemo'. 'Dove' introduces the relative/locative clause 'where'; 'la costa' is the slope/side, and 'face' here is an archaic present form of 'fare' (makes/forms). 'Di sé' is a reflexive/genitive phrase 'of itself', and 'grembo' is a noun 'bosom/lap/womb'—together 'where the slope makes of itself a bosom' (i.e., forms a hollow). Note: some supplied lemma senses for 'costa' and 'face' in the word list do not match these meanings, so senses are marked accordingly.
segments.purgatorio-7-g89: 'E là il novo giorno attenderemo' is straightforward: 'And there we will await the new day.' 'Attenderemo' is the future first-person plural of 'attendere' ('we will wait/expect'), with 'là' marking the location where they will wait.
segments.purgatorio-7-g123: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'quel nasetto' is a demonstrative + diminutive noun 'that little/snub-nosed one'. 'che' introduces a relative clause (here contracted later as c’ in the next clause); 'stretto a consiglio' is an adjectival phrase meaning 'closely bound/linked in council' (stretto = 'tight/close' modifying the relation to 'consiglio'). 'par' (3rd sing. present) = 'seems' and governs the infinitive/phrase that follows. 'con colui c’ha' = 'with that one who has...' where 'colui' = 'that one' and c’ is a contracted relative (see note) and 'ha' is present of avere. 'sì benigno aspetto' = 'so/kindly a look' (sì intensifies benigno).
segments.purgatorio-7-g124: 'morì' is the passato remoto (simple past) of morire = 'he died'. 'fuggendo' is a gerund (present participle) meaning 'fleeing/while fleeing' and here gives the manner of death. 'e' = 'and'. 'disfiorando' is another gerund from disfiorare meaning here figuratively 'deflowering' (i.e. depriving of maidenhood or, metaphorically, dishonoring). 'il giglio' is the direct object; in this context the 'giglio' likely refers to the fleur-de-lis (the emblem of France), so the verb phrase says he died while fleeing and dishonoring the lily.
segments.purgatorio-7-g125: 'guardate' is the 2nd-person-plural imperative (or present indicative used as an address) 'look' / 'see'. 'là' is a locative adverb 'there' / 'yonder'. 'come' introduces a subordinate clause 'how/as'. 'si batte il petto' is a reflexive construction: 'si' marks the action as done to oneself ('beats his breast'); 'batte' is present indicative 'beats'; 'il petto' is the direct object 'the breast/chest'. The whole phrase is an exhortation to look at how he is beating his breast.
segments.purgatorio-7-g126: 'L’altro' = 'the other one'. 'vedete' is present 2 pl. 'you see' (a parenthetical remark). 'c’ ha fatto' is a contracted relative clause: c’ stands for 'che' (who/that) and 'ha fatto' is perfect 'has made/done'. The construction 'a la guancia de la sua palma' literal 'to the cheek of his palm' is idiomatic: it means 'on his cheek with his palm' (the palm is placed against the cheek).
segments.purgatorio-7-g127: 'sospirando' is a gerund (present participle) meaning 'sighing', describing the manner in which the action of the main clause was performed; it modifies 'ha fatto'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g128: Letto here functions as a nominal/adjectival complement formed from a past participle; in context it is best understood metaphorically as 'a resting place/bed' for the cheek (i.e. 'he made his palm a bed for his cheek'). The printed lemma/senses given (from 'leggere') don't match this usage, so no provided sense fits precisely.
segments.purgatorio-7-g129: 'Padre e suocero' = 'father and father-in-law' (two nouns joined by 'e'). 'son' is the contracted plural present 'sono' = 'are'. 'del mal di Francia' is a genitive phrase 'of the evil/illness/trouble of France' (mal = 'evil/harm/illness'); the full phrase identifies them as connected with the French 'evil' (political/moral trouble).
segments.purgatorio-7-g130: 'sanno' (3rd pl. present of sapere) = 'they know'. 'la vita sua' = 'his life'. 'viziata' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'vitiated / morally corrupt / spoiled'. 'e lorda' = 'and filthy' (lorda = 'dirty/filthy'); together: 'they know his life to be corrupt and filthy.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g131: 'e quindi' = 'and therefore/so'. 'viene' (3rd sing. present of venire) = 'comes' or 'arises'. 'il duol' = 'the sorrow/pain' (duol = grief). 'che' introduces a relative clause 'that'. 'sì' = 'so/thus' (intensifier). 'li' is the clitic object pronoun 'them' (here marked in the wordlist oddly), and 'lancia' (3rd sing. present of lanciare) literally 'hurls' but here means 'strikes/impels' — 'and therefore the grief comes that so seizes/throws them.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g132: 'Quel che' is a demonstrative + relative construction meaning 'he who' or 'that which'; 'par' is present indicative ('seems'); 'sì membruto' is an adverb ('so') modifying the adjective 'membruto' ('brawny/large-limbed'); 'e che s’accorda' is a second relative clause 'and who/which accords/harmonizes', where 's’' is the reflexive pronoun (si) and 'accorda' is present indicative meaning 'agrees/tones with'. The clauses are linked: the relative 'che' after 'e' connects a second attribute to the same antecedent introduced by 'Quel'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g133: The present participle 'cantando' is used adverbially to indicate manner ('while singing' or 'in singing'), modifying the verb 's'accorda' in the previous clause; its placement as a single-word clause is a common poetic compression.
segments.purgatorio-7-g134: 'Con' is the preposition 'with'; 'colui' is the demonstrative pronoun 'that one/he' used to identify a specific person; 'dal' is the contraction 'da + il' used here to mark a characteristic ('with the' or 'of the'); 'maschio' functions adjectivally ('manly') modifying 'naso' ('nose'), so the phrase means 'with that one of the manly nose' (i.e., 'that man with the manly nose'). Word order is slightly compressed for poetic effect.
segments.purgatorio-7-g135: This clause uses past historic 'portò' ('he bore/carried') with the object 'la corda' ('the cord'); 'd'ogne valor' ('of every valor') is a genitive phrase qualifying the cord. 'Cinta' is a past participle used adjectivally ('girded') agreeing with 'la corda' but placed before it as is idiomatic in Dante: literally 'he bore the cord girded of every valor' = 'he wore the cord that encircled every virtue'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g136: This is a conditional clause: 'e se re dopo lui fosse rimaso' uses the imperfect subjunctive 'fosse' + past participle 'rimaso' to mean 'and if after him there had remained/if after him were left a king'. The verbal phrase is inverted for emphasis. The appositive 'lo giovanetto che retro a lui siede' identifies that person more precisely: 'the young man who sits behind him', where 'siede' is present indicative describing the young man's current position.
segments.purgatorio-7-g137: 'Ben andava' is an imperfect indicative ('was going/went well') describing an ongoing or habitual past action; the subject is 'il valor' (personified 'valor'). The phrase 'di vaso in vaso' is an idiom meaning 'from vessel to vessel' (i.e., passing from one bearer to another); the preposition 'di' plus the repeated 'vaso in vaso' marks the successive movement among containers/people.
segments.purgatorio-7-g138: 'Che' introduces a relative/explanatory clause: 'which cannot be said of the other heirs'. 'Non si puote dir' is an impersonal or passive construction: 'si' neutral/impersonal + archaic 'puote' (modern 'può') meaning 'it can', so 'non si puote dir' = 'one cannot say' or 'cannot be said'. 'De l’altre rede' = 'of the other heirs' ('rede' = heirs/descendants), with 'de' = 'di'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g139: Simple declarative clause: proper names 'Iacomo e Federigo' are the subjects, 'hanno' is present indicative 'they have', and 'i reami' is the direct object 'the realms' — a straightforward statement 'James and Frederick have the realms.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g140: 'Del retaggio miglior' ('of the better/best heritage') is fronted for emphasis; 'miglior' is the comparative/superlative adjective preceding the noun in a compressed poetic form. 'Nessun possiede' places 'nessun' ('no one/none') before the verb 'possiede' (present indicative) to say 'no one possesses' (i.e., 'no one holds the better inheritance').
segments.purgatorio-7-g90: Tra erto e piano is a prepositional phrase meaning 'between the steep (slope) and the level (ground)'; erto and piano function as nouns/adjectives standing in contrast. Era is the imperfect of essere used here to locate or state existence ('there was' / 'was located'). Sentiero schembo: noun + postpositive adjective (schembo = 'crooked/askew'), a typical poetic word order.
segments.purgatorio-7-g91: Che is a relative pronoun referring back to sentiero ('which'). Ne is a clitic here indicating movement 'from there' / 'along it' (it links the relative clause to the location just mentioned). Condusse is passato remoto (third singular) of condurre, 'led' or 'conducted'; in fianco de la lacca is a prepositional phrase 'onto/along the flank of the hollow'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g92: Là dove begins another locative clause 'there where…'; più ch’a mezzo is a comparative expression meaning 'more than halfway' (ch’ is the contracted che used in comparisons). Muore is present indicative of morire used metaphorically 'dies/ends/fades' and il lembo is the subject 'the edge/hem'. The verb comes after the comparative phrase, a normal poetic order.
segments.purgatorio-7-g93: A simple noun list of materials. Oro is the noun 'gold'; e is the coordinating conjunction. Argento is intended as 'silver' (the provided sense list gave a verb sense, so the noun sense is not listed); fine is an adjective after argento meaning 'fine' or 'refined' and is postpositive here for poetic effect.
segments.purgatorio-7-g94: Another paired list of colorants/pigments (in the text); grammatically it's simply two nouns joined by e. The lexical items are names of pigments/colors here (the supplied sense entries do not list the color senses, so those are marked as not applicable).
segments.purgatorio-7-g95: Indaco is a single noun (indigo) presented as another item in the list; the trailing comma continues the enumerative construction.
segments.purgatorio-7-g96: Legno lucido e sereno is a noun + two postpositive adjectives 'polished/bright' and 'serene/clear' modifying legno ('wood'); e links the two adjectives. Adjectives placed after the noun is a frequent poetic ordering.
segments.purgatorio-7-g97: Fresco smeraldo is an adjectival phrase 'fresh emerald'; in l'ora che si fiacca is a temporal subordinate clause 'in the hour when it weakens/wanes'. Si is the reflexive/clitic used with fiacca (intransitive sense 'becomes weak'); fiacca is present tense (poetic form) from fiaccare used here intransitively 'grows faint'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g98: Da l’erba e da li fior is a repeated prepositional phrase 'from the grass and from the flowers', with the archaic li as the plural article. This marks source/origin for what follows.
segments.purgatorio-7-g99: Dentr’ a quel seno posti: dentr’ (dentro) 'within', a quel seno 'in that bosom/embrace', posti is the past participle 'placed' agreeing with the omitted plural subject (the flowers/grass), used predicatively. Word order (preposition + article + noun + participle) is compact and poetic.
segments.purgatorio-7-g100: Ciascun saria di color vinto is a conditional statement: ciascun 'each one', saria is a conditional (would be) from sariare, di color vinto 'of subdued/overcome color' — a predicative phrase describing the hypothetical appearance. The conditional mood here expresses a descriptive comparison.
segments.purgatorio-7-g101: Come dal suo maggiore è vinto il meno is a comparative simile: come 'as', dal suo maggiore 'by its greater', è vinto 'is overcome/surpassed' (passive/state), il meno 'the lesser'. The participle vinto functions to express the subordinate relation of being subdued/surpassed.
segments.purgatorio-7-g102: Non avea pur natura ivi dipinto: non + imperfect avea gives the negative past/state 'nature had not…'; pur (from pure) is an adverb here meaning 'even/alone' or 'simply'. Ivi is a locative 'there' and dipinto is a past participle 'painted' used as the complement of aveva.
segments.purgatorio-7-g103: Ma introduces the contrasting clause. Di soavità di mille odori is an ablative/partitive expression 'from the sweetness of a thousand scents' giving the cause/source. Vi (there) precedes the imperfect facea ('made' / 'was making'), and uno incognito e indistinto is the direct object/predicative result 'one unknown and indistinct thing'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g104: Salve is an interjection/greeting 'Hail!' quoted as part of speech. It stands alone and is marked by the opening quote in the original.
segments.purgatorio-7-g105: Regina continues the quoted address 'Queen'. In sul verde e 'n su' fiori is a locative phrase 'on the green and on its flowers' (in + sul = stylistic combination; 'su'' is an archaic form of 'suo' with the following elided vowel). Quindi introduces the next action 'then'; vider/vidi construction follows: vidi (I saw) + infinitive (seder 'to sit') is a common catenative structure meaning 'I saw [them] sit', and cantando is a present participle 'singing' modifying anime (souls), the direct object of vidi.
segments.purgatorio-7-g106: Che begins a relative clause modifying the preceding souls (or their action). Per la valle 'through the valley' is a circumstantial phrase; non parean is the imperfect 'did not seem/appear', and di fuori is an adverbial phrase 'from outside' or 'externally'. The clause describes how they appeared as they passed.
segments.purgatorio-7-g107: 'Prima che' is a temporal subordinating conjunction meaning 'before'; the article '’l' is the elided form of 'il' (the). 'poco sole' literally 'little sun' — a poetic image for the low/late sun. 'omai' = 'by now' or 'already' (adverb). 's’annidi' is the reflexive verb 'annidarsi' in the present subjunctive (s' + annidi) used after 'Prima che' to mark an action expected to happen; literally 'before it nests' = before the sun sets.
segments.purgatorio-7-g108: Main clause: 'cominciò' is passato remoto (literary past) meaning 'began'. '’l Mantoan' = 'the Mantuan' (epithet for Virgil); the relative clause 'che ci avea vòlti' follows and modifies him. 'avea vòlti' is auxiliary 'avea' (imperfect) + past participle 'vòlti' = a pluperfect-like sense 'had turned (us)'. 'ci' is the clitic object 'us'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g109: 'tra color' = 'among those (people)'. 'non vogliate' is present subjunctive (second-person plural) of 'volere' used here as a prohibitive/request ('do not wish/ask'); this is classical/poetic usage of the subjunctive with an imperative force. 'ch’io vi guidi' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'che' with the present subjunctive 'guidi' (first-person singular): 'that I guide you'. 'vi' is the clitic indirect object 'you'. Note the final word 'guidi' here is a verb (to guide) and not the proper name despite the lemma given.
segments.purgatorio-7-g110: Literal order: 'Di questo balzo' = 'From/about this ledge/step'; 'meglio' (= 'more clearly/better') modifies the verb. 'li atti e 'volti' are direct objects (the gestures/acts and the faces), with the article 'li'. 'conoscerete' is the future indicative (second-person plural) 'you will know/recognize'. 'voi' is the explicit subject pronoun. 'di tutti quanti' = 'of all those/all of them' (tutti quanti = everyone/all that).
segments.purgatorio-7-g111: Relative clause introduced by 'che' referring back to 'tutti quanti'. 'ne la lama' = poetic/inverted phrasing meaning 'in the pool/marsh' (ne + article + noun used adverbially: 'in the marsh'). 'giù' = 'down'. 'tra essi' = 'among them'. 'accolti' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'gathered/harboured/accepted' describing the people; word order is compressed because of the verse form.
segments.purgatorio-7-g112: 'Colui' = 'that one/he who' starts a relative construction. 'più siede alto' = 'sits higher/more lofty' (più intensifies 'siede alto'). 'fa sembianti' = 'makes pretences/put on appearances'; 'd’aver negletto' is 'of having neglected' where 'aver' (infinitive) + past participle 'negletto' forms a perfect infinitive. 'ciò che far dovea' = 'what he should have done' — 'far' is the infinitive 'to do' and 'dovea' is the imperfect of dovere ('ought/should'), so the clause expresses obligation in the past.
segments.purgatorio-7-g113: 'e che' continues the description: 'and who'. 'non move' = 'does not move/does not open' (present indicative, 3rd singular). 'bocca a li altrui canti' literally 'his mouth to the others' songs' — i.e. he does not sing along or does not respond to others' calls; 'a' marks the relation to 'canti'.
segments.purgatorio-7-g114: Short, nominative identification: 'Rodolfo imperador fu' uses past 'fu' (passato remoto or literary perfect) meaning 'Rudolf was emperor.' The inversion (name + title + verb) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.purgatorio-7-g115: Relative clause 'che potea sanar le piaghe...' — 'potea' is the imperfect 'was able/could', here used to mean 'could have' in the sense of capacity. 'sanar' (infinitive) = 'to heal'. 'le piaghe' = 'the wounds/afflictions'. 'c’ hanno' is a contracted relative 'che hanno' ('that have'); 'Italia morta' = 'Italy dead' (morta as adjective). Together: 'who could (and would) have healed the wounds that have (made) Italy dead.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g116: 'sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that'). 'tardi' = 'late' or 'slowly'; 'per altri' = 'by others' or 'because of others'; 'si ricrea' is reflexive present ('is restored/refreshed') with 'si' marking a passive/reflexive sense. The clause means that, as a result, Italy is late to be restored by others.
segments.purgatorio-7-g117: L’ (contraction of il) + altro = 'the other'. Che introduces a relative clause modifying l'altro. Ne la vista is a prepositional phrase: ne = 'in/there' and la vista = 'the sight/presence', so together 'in that sight/there in the presence'. Lui is the personal object 'him' and conforta is present indicative 'comforts' (the relative clause uses present for vivid description). Word order places the object lui before the verb, which is normal in Italian subordinate clauses.
segments.purgatorio-7-g118: Resse is the passato remoto of reggere here meaning 'he ruled/governed' (an older poetic past). La terra is the direct object 'the land'. Dove introduces a locative relative clause 'where'; l'acqua nasce = 'the water springs' (nasce in a present/general sense 'springs up'). Che begins a further specification identifying that water, and Molta is the river-name being specified; the phrase che Molta in Albia is elliptical/poetic (the verb for 'flows' is omitted), so read as 'which (the) Molta (takes) into the Albia.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g119: E = 'and'. Albia is the proper-name subject (the river Albia). In mar = 'into the sea' (mar = mare). Ne is a clitic pronoun referring back to the water just mentioned (i.e. 'it/of it'), and porta is present indicative 'carries' or 'bears' — the present here gives a vivid, descriptive effect and completes the sequence 'Molta into Albia, and Albia carries it to the sea.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g120: Ottacchero is a proper name; ebbe is the passato remoto of avere 'had' used idiomatically here ('had the name') so 'was called'; nome = 'name'. The whole phrase means 'he was called Ottacchero.'
segments.purgatorio-7-g121: E = 'and'. Ne le fasce = 'in the swaddling-bands' (ne = 'in/within', le fasce = swaddling bands/clothes) — an idiom meaning 'as an infant' or 'even in his infancy'. Fu is passato remoto of essere 'was'. Meglio assai = 'much better' (meglio = better, assai = very/much). Che here is comparative 'than'. Vincislao suo figlio barbuto = 'Vincislao, his bearded son' (barbuto is a postpositive adjective describing the son).
segments.purgatorio-7-g122: Cui is a relative pronoun 'whom' (referring to Vincislao) and functions as the object of the verb. Lussuria e ozio = 'lust and idleness' (two nominal subjects). Pasce is the present tense of pascere 'feeds' (here 'nourish' or 'are feeding him'); the construction literally means 'whom lust and idleness feed.'

sentences.purgatorio-7-s6: Like one who suddenly sees some thing before himself and marvels thereat, who both believes and does not, saying, "She is..."
sentences.purgatorio-7-s12: So he seemed to him; then he lowered his eyelids, and humbly returned toward him, and embraced him where the younger one clings.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s1: After the courteous and joyful welcomes had been repeated three or four times, Sordel drew back and said, “You — who are you?”
sentences.purgatorio-7-s3: Before the souls worthy of ascending to God had turned toward this mountain, my bones were buried by Octavian.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s4: I am Virgil; and I lost Heaven for no other wickedness than for not having faith.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s13: “O glory of the Latins,” he said, “through whom our tongue showed what it could, O eternal honor of the place whence I came, by what merit or by what grace do you show yourself to me?”
sentences.purgatorio-7-s14: If I am worthy to hear your words, tell me whether you come from Hell, and from what cloister.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s15: “Through all the circles of the doleful realm,” he answered, “I have come from this side; a power of heaven moved me, and with it I come.”
sentences.purgatorio-7-s16: Not by doing, but by not doing I have lost the chance to see the high Sun that you desire and that I recognized too late.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s21: But see already how the day is declining, and one cannot go up at night; therefore it is well to think of a pleasant place to stay.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s22: There are souls a little off to the right here; if you consent to me, I will bring you to them, and it will not be without pleasure that you shall be made known.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s49: The one who sits lowest among these, looking upward, is William the Marquis, by whom both Alessandria and his war make Monferrato and Canavese weep.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s17: There is a place down there not sorrowful from torments, but of darkness alone; where the lamentations do not sound like cries of woe, but are sighs.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s18: There I stand with the innocent little ones, bitten by the teeth of death before they could be subject to human guilt; there I stand with those who did not clothe themselves in the three holy virtues, and, without vice, knew the other virtues and followed them all.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s19: But if you know and can, give us some sign by which we may come more quickly to where Purgatory has its rightful beginning.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s20: He answered: "There certainly is no place; it is permitted for me to go up and around; as far as I can go, I will come close to you as a guide."
sentences.purgatorio-7-s25: Whoever wished to climb at night, would he be hindered by others, or would it not be that he could not?
sentences.purgatorio-7-s27: And good Sordello rubbed his finger on the ground, saying: "See?"
sentences.purgatorio-7-s28: You would not pass this single ridge after the sun has set; not that any other thing would give trouble in going up there, but the nocturnal darkness; that, together with lack of power, entangles the desire.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s45: Human probity seldom reappears along the branches; and this is what he who gives it wishes, so that it may be called after him.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s46: My words go also to the long‑nosed one no less than to the other, Pier, who sings with him, wherefore Apulia and Provence already lament.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s47: The plant of his seed is so much smaller, than (it is) that Costanza still boasts of a husband more than Beatrice and Margherita.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s48: See the king of the simple life sitting there alone, Henry of England: this one has a better outcome in his branches.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s29: One could very well go back down with her and stroll around the slope, wandering,
sentences.purgatorio-7-s30: Then my lord, almost admiring, said, 'Lead us on, then, to that place where you say one can have delight in dwelling.'
sentences.purgatorio-7-s31: Having advanced a little along the ridges, we were there when I perceived that the mountain was bare, in the manner the ravines strip it here.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s32: 'There,' said that shade, 'we will go from there to where the slope makes a womb of itself; and there we will await the new day.'
sentences.purgatorio-7-s41: And that little snub-nosed one, who, closely allied in counsel, seems to be with the man who has such a benign expression,
sentences.purgatorio-7-s42: The other, you see, has, with the palm of his hand, made his cheek (rest) there, sighing.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s43: His father and father-in-law belong to France’s evil: they know his life is tainted and filthy, and therefore comes the sorrow that so plunges them.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s44: He who seems so brawny and who harmonizes, while singing, with that one of the manly nose, bore girded the cord of every valor; and if after him a king had remained, the young man who sits behind him, valor passed well from vessel to vessel — which cannot be said of the other heirs; James and Frederick have the realms; of the better heritage no one is possessed.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s33: Between steep and level there was a crooked path that led us along the flank of the hollow, where the edge dies more than halfway.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s34: Gold and fine silver, cochineal and white, indigo, polished and serene wood, fresh emerald at the hour it grows faint; from the grass and the flowers placed within that bosom it made one unknown and indistinct thing, each would be of subdued color, as the lesser is subdued by its greater.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s35: Nature alone had not painted it there, but the sweetness of a thousand scents there made one unknown and indistinct thing.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s36: 'Hail, Queen' upon the green and on its flowers; then I saw souls sitting there, singing, which through the valley did not seem to be from outside.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s37: "Before the little sun sinks," began the Mantuan who had turned us, "do not ask that I lead you among those."
sentences.purgatorio-7-s38: From this ledge you will more clearly recognize the gestures and faces of all those who are gathered down in the marsh among them.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s39: He who sits highest and makes a show of having neglected what he ought to have done, and who does not move his mouth to others' songs, was Emperor Rudolf, who could have healed the wounds that have laid Italy low, so that she is slow to be restored by others.
sentences.purgatorio-7-s40: The other, who comforts him there in sight, ruled the land where the water springs — the Molta flows into the Albia, and the Albia carries it to the sea: he was called Ottacchero, and even in his swaddling-bands he was far better than his bearded son Vincislao, whom lust and idleness nourish.
